relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Battle fear is answered by remembering the Exodus rather than worshiping superior weapons.
A priest speaks before commanders take control.
The army is addressed at the level of the frightened heart.
Deliverance is attributed to YHWH rather than military strength.
A new house is allowed to become a home before war claims its builder.
A vineyard is allowed to bear joy for the one who planted it.
A promised marriage is allowed to become a shared life.
The fearful may return home rather than be punished into courage.
Only after exemptions are spoken are commanders appointed.
The first approach to a distant city is called peace.
Yet the offered peace becomes forced labor, revealing peace without freedom as domination.
Refusal opens siege.
The city's males are killed after defeat.
Women, children, animals, and goods are taken as spoil.
This pattern is assigned to distant cities.
Nearby peoples receive the chapter's most absolute command: leave no breath alive.
Six named peoples are placed under total destruction.
Religious difference is used as the rationale for annihilation.
Even during siege, fruit trees are not enemies and must remain alive.
Only non-food trees may become siege material within the ancient war system.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 20 cannot be purified into a peace text. It contains compassionate exemptions and ecological restraint inside a structure of forced labor, killing, captivity, and genocide. The usable inheritance is clear: protect civilian life, let fear go home, preserve food-bearing trees—and reject the war system that surrounds them.
